currentAppMatch.title: Great Earth
currentAppMatch.creditLine: Collection of National Gallery Singapore. © Goh Beng Kwan.
currentAppMatch.description: Born in 1937 in Indonesia, Goh Beng Kwan migrated with his family to Singapore in 1945. In the 1950s, he studied Chinese ink painting with Chen Wen Hsi. In 1963, he studied at the Art Students' League in New York where he was first introduced to collage art and Abstract Expressionism. Goh was awarded the inaugural UOB Painting of the Year First Prize in 1982 and the Cultural Medallion in 1989.Goh is noted for his collage works with contain an “oriental perspective”. Inspired by nature, environment and his ethnic Chinese heritage, his works relate to sensory impressions of the places he has visited. Goh’s works are mainly mixed media pieces which use natural and man-made materials.

currentAppMatch.title: Isorhythm I
currentAppMatch.creditLine: Gift of Fanny, Jenny and Alice Yap. Collection of National Gallery Singapore.
currentAppMatch.description: Born in Singapore, Arthur Yap (1943-2006) painted more frequently only after he graduated from the University of Singapore in 1965. A self-taught artist, he learnt through art magazines, exhibitions and trips to Europe in the 1970s. At the same time, he participated actively in the local art scene, holding six solo exhibitions and exhibiting in numerous group shows from 1968. In 1972, he was one of the Singapore representatives to the Adelaide Festival of Arts. Recognized as one of the country’s foremost poets with three published collections of poems, Yap was awarded the Cultural Medallion for literature by the Singapore government in 1983.Yap’s early preference for clean, hard edges is evident in ‘Isorhythm I’ even as undulating bands weave across the five segments of the composition. This painting was featured in an exhibition organized by the Singapore Art Society in 1969 to commemorate the 150th anniversary of the founding of Singapore and 20th anniversary of the Society.

currentAppMatch.title: Born Free
currentAppMatch.medium: Acrylic, oil and spray paint on canvas
currentAppMatch.description: Born in 1954 in Singapore, Ng Yak Hwee is a graduate of Singapore’s former Nanyang University (Botany major).  An accomplished painter and sculptor, Ng received the 1987 Silver Medal from the Salon des Artistes Français, and won First Prize (Figurative category) in the 1992 UOB Painting of the Year Competition.On the cusp of a new decade, Goh’s paintings began to swing away from the purely abstract deliverance that his work had acquired by the late 1980s, to once again include representational elements. ‘Born Free’ (expanding on a theme explored in Goh’s ‘Katuring the Sea’ series) and ‘Spring Time’ still reflect Goh’s preoccupation with travel-inspired Nature themes.  However, in the former, Goh has sought to merge his Western painting technique with traditional Chinese painting pictorial arrangement.  The latter heralds the beginning of Spring, the half-formed blossoms emerging from quiet hibernation, symbolising man’s spirit responding in tune with the changing seasons.

currentAppMatch.title: Figure Drawing
currentAppMatch.description:
Tang Da Wu, born in 1943, is widely regarded as the central figure in the development of ‘alternative’ art in Singapore. A graduate from Goldsmith College, University of London with a Master of Art, he led a group of younger artists to establish The Artists Village in Singapore in 1988, where performance, installation and painting took place. Since the late 1990s, Tang has been working on community projects that deal with memory, history as well as environmental issues. In 1999, Tang was awarded the Arts and Culture Prize at the 10th Fukuoka Asian Culture Prizes and in 2007, he was one of four artists who represented Singapore at the Venice Biennale.

In these two ink works of a silhouette of a woman, both titled ‘Figure Drawing’, the curves are emphasized by confident broad strokes which remind one of a well-executed calligraphy.

Ong, whose first solo exhibition in 1979 featured works from his 1978 trek to the Himalayas, became a full-time artist in 1985. currentAppMatch.description: Born in 1954 in Singapore, Ng Yak Hwee is a graduate of Singapore’s former Nanyang University (Botany major).  An accomplished painter and sculptor, Ng received the 1987 Silver Medal from the Salon des Artistes Français, and won First Prize (Figurative category) in the 1992 UOB Painting of the Year Competition.Executed after the exhibition but stylistically and thematically similar to the paintings of Goh’s third solo show “Being with Nature”, ‘Autumn Mist’ and ‘Poem's Rock’ depict Goh’s familiar expressive bold strokes, abstract forms and subtly layered colours used to represent (real and imagined) scenes from nature. These works cap Goh’s evolving style during a decade of voluminous output and artistic development, in which the artist became primarily known as a landscape painter, but one who had gradually shifted into producing abstract paintings without any representational references by the late 1980s.

currentAppMatch.title: Fu Lu Shou
currentAppMatch.description: Vincent Leow (1961) has carved out an important role in Singapore's contemporary art development. He works in a wide range of genres, from performances, installations and sculptures, to digital and mixed-media works. One of the early members of The Artists Village, founded in 1988, Leow's art has been about emphasizing the 'process' in art where he constantly challenges how people consume and understand art; either through playful or antagonistic means. Leow's role as an educator has also inspired today's generation of young contemporary artists who are interested in works of a similar vein.'Fu Lu Shuo' could be regarded as a study as well as precedence to Vincent''s oeuvre representing fictionalized narratives about the composite of different cultures. This etching which was produced in 1987, while Leow was pursuing his Diploma at LASALLE College of the Arts, may well be a significant step towards his venture in interrogating contemporary issues, interweaving them with myths and fables.

currentAppMatch.title: Flowing Series
currentAppMatch.description: Born in 1946, Chng Seok Tin graduated from the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1972.  In 1980, she took up post-graduate studies in printmaking which was followed by a year's practice under S.W. Hayter at Atelier 17, Paris. Since the 1980s, Chng has been instrumental in promoting interest in printmaking and has held more than 20 solo exhibitions. In May 2005, Chng was the first Singaporean to hold a solo exhibition at the United Nations Headquarter and was awarded the Cultural Medallion in the same year.Chng’s works are often inspired by nature and water, one of her favourite motifs, has been studied and depicted extensively. ‘Flowing Series’ showcases four prints of water in different light, mood and agitation.

currentAppMatch.title: A scene in Bali
currentAppMatch.medium: Chinese ink and colour on paper
currentAppMatch.description: Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Reflecting his background as a calligrapher, lines rather than tones are paramount in Lim’s art. In this painting of Bali, delicate linework is used in the depiction of the magnificent tree, its spreading branches and the ubiquitous shrines that dot the island landscape.

currentAppMatch.title: Sungei Road
currentAppMatch.description: Born in 1931, Choo Keng Kwang is widely recognized for realistic renditions of landscape, animals and nature in the oil medium. Sympathetic to local working classes, Choo features them in many of his works. Graduating in 1953 from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA), he worked as a teacher, eventually returning to NAFA to helm the Art Education Department in 1984. Choo has since held many solo exhibitions and has participated in group art exhibitions in Southeast Asia, Japan, Europe and USA. Since the 1950s, Choo has received many awards for his many contributions to art and education, among which is the Public Service Medal (PBM) in 1976.Sungei Road is home of one of the oldest flea markets in Singapore. Popularly known as Thieves Market, street peddlers selling second-hand items line the lanes near Rochor Canal. Although the market had its heyday in the 1980s it is still frequented these days by bargain-hunters. This painting by Choo takes an unusual view of Sungei Road as it does not show the street peddlers but instead focuses on the architecture of a two-storey building on that street.

currentAppMatch.description: Born in 1946, Chng Seok Tin graduated from the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1972.  In 1980, she took up post-graduate studies in printmaking which was followed by a year's practice under S.W. Hayter at Atelier 17, Paris. In May 2005, Chng was the first Singaporean to hold a solo exhibition at the United Nations Headquarter and was awarded the Cultural Medallion in the same year.

currentAppMatch.title: Winter in Iowa
currentAppMatch.description: Each of these 64 squares corresponds to a hexagram in the ancient Chinese divination text I-Ching (易经), also known as the Book of Changes. A person seeking guidance from the I-Ching tosses three coins six times. Each toss determines one line in a hexagram, which is then looked up in the text and interpreted to form the answer to their question. Chng similarly created each square here according to her reading of the corresponding hexagram in the I-Ching. Based on her interpretations, she used plain materials like twine, sand and wire to create elemental shapes and forms. Chng began incorporating the philosophy of the I-Ching into her work while she was in New Mexico State University, and even consulted the text herself to resolve personal issues. The work featured the 64 squares laid on the floor when it was first exhibited in New Mexico in 1983, and then at the National Museum Art Gallery in 1989. In 1992, Chng decided to mount the squares on a black and white canvas, symbolising the interplay of chance and the Taoist concept of balancing yin and yang in life.

currentAppMatch.title: Dancer
currentAppMatch.description: One of the most prolific and talented artists in Singapore, Ng Eng Teng produced more than 300 sculptures over a span of 40 years. These included large-scale public sculptures to life-sized and smaller sculptures. His art education started in Singapore as a painting student at Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA), before continuing his studies in industrial pottery in North Staffordshire College of Technology, England. He went on to study studio pottery in Farnham School of Art and subsequently became a designer in a pottery factory in Ireland for a few years. These various stints equipped him with the necessary technical vocabulary to deal with the 3-dimensional form.From the Dancer series which dates back to around 1990, ‘Dancer’ continues Ng’s investigation into re-looking at the human torso. In this series, although the torso has been reduced and simplified, the figure is still the central preoccupation. However beyond examining formal qualities, there is an attempt by Ng to capture the essence of a dancer in motion.

currentAppMatch.title: Musician
currentAppMatch.creditLine: Collection of National Gallery Singapore. © Yeh Toh Yen
currentAppMatch.description: Born in Fuzhou, China, Yeh Chi Wei (1915-1981) is included among a group referred to as Singapore’s “first generation artists”.  Graduating from Shanghai’s Xinhua Academy of Fine Arts in 1936, Yeh worked as an art teacher throughout Malaya and Singapore until 1964, while an active member of various art groups. He was especially noted in having started a series of painting trips since 1960 to various Southeast Asian and Asian countries under the Ten Men Group, as well as the Ten Men Art Exhibitions after such trips.The five paintings, ‘Portrait of a Dyak Lady’, ‘The Dyak Plays the Musical Instrument’, ‘Drummer’, ‘Musician’ and ‘Untitled’ (Accession No. P-0752), are examples of Yeh’s experimentation with incorporating diverse Western and Eastern influences and interests into the treatment of his figurative subjects.  His elongated figures are rendered in black, framed by textured fields of muted colour.  Yeh’s quiet and thoughtful approach brought to each painting, distils the subject matter down to its essence both in terms of its narrative, composition and painterly technique.  His depiction of indigenous cultures offers a world rich with evocative inner meaning.

currentAppMatch.title: Woman Supporting the Roof
currentAppMatch.description: Tang Da Wu, born in 1943, is widely regarded as the central figure in the development of ‘alternative’ art in Singapore. A graduate from Goldsmith College, University of London with a Master of Art, he led a group of younger artists to establish The Artists Village in Singapore in 1988, where performance, installation and painting took place. Since the late 1990s, Tang has been working on community projects that deal with memory, history as well as environmental issues. In 1999, Tang was awarded the Arts and Culture Prize at the 10th Fukuoka Asian Culture Prizes and in 2007, he was one of four artists who represented Singapore at the Venice Biennale.

currentAppMatch.title: Mother and Twins
currentAppMatch.description: One of the most prolific and talented artists in Singapore, Ng Eng Teng produced more than 300 sculptures over a span of 40 years. These included large-scale public sculptures to life-sized and smaller sculptures. His art education started in Singapore as a painting student at Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA), before continuing his studies in industrial pottery in North Staffordshire College of Technology, England. He went on to study studio pottery in Farnham School of Art and subsequently became a designer in a pottery factory in Ireland for a few years. These various stints equipped him with the necessary technical vocabulary to deal with the 3-dimensional form.Ng, who dealt primarily with the human figure and humanistic themes in his art, believed that humanity began with the family foundation and the dynamic relationships between the parents and their children. ‘Mother and Twins’ is another example of his long-standing investigation into the mother-child relationship. Here the anatomy is reduced into an S-shaped curve, reminiscent of the umbilical cord that sustains a child during pregnancy.

stale.medium: Watercolour on paper, bound into linen-covered album
stale.description: This hand-painted album contains 54 images of the ceremonial dress of the Nguyen imperial court in the early 20th century. While the album appears to be an affirmation of court tradition, the court was actually dominated by the French colonial administration at this time, and was undergoing significant change. It is likely that the album was intended to catalogue the costumes of the court for a foreign patron. The realistic style of the images suggests the artist’s familiarity with Western painting conventions in a period before the establishment of colonial art schools in Vietnam.

stale.dimensions:
Frame measure: Frame: 96.3 x 96.3 x 2.5 cm
Image measure: Image: 81.0 x 81.0 cm
stale.creditLine: Collection of National Gallery Singapore. Image courtesy of Jaafar Latiff's family.
stale.description: The Batik 87/88 series demonstrates Jaafar’s practice of painting several canvases at a time. This allowed him to streamline the batik making process, which involves multiple applications of waxing, dyeing, and rinsing. He also applied dye directly on the canvas as opposed to the traditional method of soaking the canvas in a dye bath. This series displays more exuberant canting strokes and colour schemes than Jaafar’s earlier Wandering series. The harmony and contrast of the complementary colour combinations in the paintings accentuates the rhythmic quality of the series. For example, the pairing of yellow with red-purple and blue-purple combinations in Batik 15 - 87/88 creates a sense of undulating movement. This is even more evident when the paintings are displayed sequentially as seen here. Art historian TK Sabapathy described the Batik 87/88 series in a 1988 review as an “outcome of an artistic practice that is gaining in maturity.”

stale.title: Layers of Time
stale.medium: Oil and mixed media on canvas
stale.dimensions:
Frame measure: 176.8 x 143.5 x 3.7 cm
Image measure: 169.8 x 137.0 cm
stale.description: Time is a continuous concern in Lin’s work and formed the basis of her exhibition From Time to Time at National Museum Art Gallery (NMAG) and a volume of poetry with the same title in 1991. Time manifests in different ways across her practice from Lin’s penchant for doing things at speed and being ahead of the curve, to her philosophical expressions of history and memory, or the pacing of her music compositions. Layers of Time and Sizzling Bubbles (from the Aqua series) use the metaphor of water to capture transient moments, like birds amidst gathering storm clouds. In contrast, In Appreciation of Time and Ahead of Time (from the Time series) are contemplative, reflecting on our fleeting human existence against the infinity of cosmological time or the accelerated feel of technological time.

currentAppMatch.title: Kenyah Lady
currentAppMatch.creditLine: Gift of the Loke Wan Tho Collection. Collection of National Gallery Singapore.
currentAppMatch.description: Cheong Soo Pieng was born in Amoy, China in 1917. He studied at the Amoy Art Academy and the Xinhua Academy of Fine Arts in Shanghai from 1933 to 1936. In 1946, Cheong settled in Singapore and taught at the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts until 1961. Together with fellow artists Chen Wen Hsi, Chen Chong Swee and Liu Kang, they went to Bali, Indonesia in 1952 in search of new inspiration and subject matter. Cheong had a dominant influence on the development of modern art in Singapore and is regarded as one of Singapore’s pioneer artists. His innovative experimentations towards developing his stylistic oeuvre had a great influence on his students, many of whom later became established artists in the region. For his contributions in art, he was awarded the Meritorious Service Medal by the Singapore government in 1962. Cheong passed away in 1983 in Singapore.Cheong was a well-travelled artist and had visited regional places such as Bali, Borneo and Sarawak to seek out new subject matter. Here is a portrait of a Kenyah lady, the indigenous people of Borneo. Rendered in Chinese ink, Cheong captures the most characteristic feature of the Kenyah women; their extremely long earlobes, distended with heavy earrings.

sourceRecord.title: Kenyah Lady
sourceRecord.creditLine: Gift of the Loke Wan Tho Collection. Collection of National Gallery Singapore.

currentAppMatch.title: Balinese Selling Toddy
currentAppMatch.creditLine: Gift of the Loke Wan Tho Collection. Collection of National Gallery Singapore.
currentAppMatch.description:
The smooth and highly pigmented appearance of this painting can be attributed to Cheong’s choice to paint on the thin and highly absorbent support of Chinese paper. Rather than affixing it to a vertical hanging scroll according to the conventions of Chinese ink painting, this fragile paper was placed on another sheet of paper, which then allowed the work to be framed in this rectangular format.

By framing the artwork in a format commonly associated with the display of oil paintings—as well as depicting a specifically Southeast Asian subject—Cheong melded different cultural sources together in both stylistic and material ways.
